text: The surgeon inspects the surgical field, revealing numerous omental adhesions. Using a hook instrument, the surgeon performs adhesiolysis by hooking the tissue, visually assessing the area, and applying electrocautery. The pneumoperitoneum aids in dissecting the tissue planes, allowing precise separation without collateral damage.

The surgeon continues adhesiolysis in the right upper abdomen, carefully bringing the colon down and moving it laterally to detach the lateral attachments of the omentum. The adhesions are being dissected further along the edge of the liver.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction techniques to continue adhesiolysis near the gallbladder, revealing fluid-laden tissue indicative of acute cholecystitis. The omental adhesions on the lateral aspects are fully dissected.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the medial aspect, carefully bringing down adhesions to prevent tearing the Glisson's capsule and avoid bleeding. The hook dissection is completed, and the suction cannula is used to open tissue planes in the area of Calot's triangle, clearly delineating the anatomy without the use of energy devices.

The surgeon uses a hook to perform monopolar dissection in the area of Calot's triangle, applying blend current mode to carefully dissect layer by layer. The dissection focuses on identifying the vessel from the cystic artery supplying the cystic duct.

The surgeon uses monopolar electrosurgery to coagulate the vessel, creating space between the cystic artery and cystic duct. The left hand instrument is opened in a splaying technique to facilitate precise dissection between these structures. The critical view of safety is identified once the dissection is complete.

The surgeon completes the posterior dissection, reflecting the gallbladder upwards by at least 2.5 centimeters to achieve the critical view of safety. The cystic artery is visibly clipped and cut, creating a clear window in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a hemlock clip to secure the cystic duct, which appears white in color. Two hemlock clips are visibly positioned on the duct, and the surgeon milks out small stones upwards from the duct during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies a large titanium clip to the cystic duct after milking it upwards. The cystic duct is then cut evenly to prevent slippage of the stump, ensuring secure closure.

The surgeon reflects the gallbladder using the flagging technique on both the right and left sides, employing the hook, look, and cook principle to carefully apply electrosurgical current. The final dissection is completed in the area of the gallbladder fundus.

The surgeon safely removes the gallbladder from the fossa without spillage, placing it in a retrieval bag. A gentle saline lavage is performed to identify any bleeding spots, revealing low-pressure, low-volume bleeding. A small piece of Surgicel is applied to manage the bleeding. A drain, in the form of an NG tube, is placed due to the acute inflammatory condition of the gallbladder. The specimen is removed through the widened epigastric port, and a small metadine gauze is used to clean the port area.
